name: Malayan Emergency
summary: The Malayan Emergency was a counterinsurgency war from mid-June 1948 to 31 July 1960 between the Malayan Communist Party/Malayan National Liberation Army and the Federation of Malaya backed by British and Commonwealth security forces. Government statistics compiled by Karl Hack give 6,711 insurgents killed, 1,865 security forces killed, 2,473 civilians killed, and 810 civilians missing. If the missing civilians are treated as dead, the commonly cited precise total is 11,859 deaths.

sides.name: Federation of Malaya / British Empire and Commonwealth security forces

military_killed.notes: Includes security forces killed during the Emergency. Hack's government-statistics table gives 1,865, broken down as 1,346 police and 519 army/Commonwealth deaths; other sources give 1,856 or rounded figures around 1,800.

breakdown.victims_group: unarmed civilians at Batang Kali / Sungai Rimoh
breakdown.notes: A Scots Guards patrol shot and killed 24 civilians in December 1948; later UK litigation centered on the refusal to hold a public inquiry.

breakdown.victims_group: civilians killed by British military/security forces outside Batang Kali
breakdown.notes: AOAV identifies 38 confirmed civilian killings by British military forces in total, including Batang Kali; it also flags 56 additional fatal shootings as suspicious. Subtracting Batang Kali leaves 14 confirmed non-Batang Kali deaths and up to 70 if suspicious cases are included.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: Low/point are confirmed British military civilian killings as compiled by AOAV; high adds AOAV's 56 suspicious fatal shootings. This is not a complete perpetrator-coded total for every government-side action, especially police actions.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: rural Chinese squatters, New Village residents, labor-line populations, and Orang Asli communities
deaths_from_actions.notes: Forced resettlement moved roughly 500,000 to over 560,000 people into New Villages, and over 1 million if defensible labor lines are included. Qualitative accounts describe coercion, deprivation, disease, and death, especially among Orang Asli, but no reliable consolidated mortality estimate was found; therefore these deaths are not added numerically to the grand total.

sides.name: Malayan Communist Party / Malayan National Liberation Army

military_killed.notes: Hack's government-statistics table gives 6,711 insurgents killed. Cross-checks give 6,697, 6,698, or rounded figures around 6,700.

breakdown.victims_group: civilian population of Malaya, including estate managers, suspected informers, workers, and villagers
breakdown.notes: Residual attribution: official figures list 2,473 civilians killed and 810 missing; sources say civilian casualties were mostly inflicted by insurgents, but the official table is not a complete perpetrator-coded dataset. Low subtracts confirmed+suspicious British/security-force civilian killings; high treats missing civilians as dead and subtracts only confirmed British killings.

breakdown.victims_group: European estate managers at Sungai Siput
breakdown.notes: Three European estate managers were killed in Perak on 16 June 1948, the immediate trigger for the emergency declaration.

breakdown.victims_group: police, auxiliary police, family members, and civilians at Bukit Kepong
breakdown.notes: Royal Malaysian Police commemorative accounts give 25 total dead in the 23 February 1950 Bukit Kepong attack, including police, auxiliary police, six family members, and two civilians; classification between combatant and civilian varies for auxiliary police and family members.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: The point estimate of 2,435 is a residual calculation: 2,473 official civilian killed minus 38 confirmed British military killings. It should not be read as an official perpetrator-coded MCP/MNLA figure.

totals.notes: Military total sums security-force and insurgent deaths from the low/high source range. Civilian low is the official 2,473 killed; civilian high adds 810 missing civilians as presumed dead. Hack's precise government-statistics roll-up is 11,859 total deaths when the missing civilians are counted as dead.

key_atrocities.name: Batang Kali / Sungai Rimoh massacre
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Federation of Malaya / British Empire and Commonwealth security forces
key_atrocities.notes: Scots Guards patrol shot and killed 24 civilians in December 1948; UK courts later addressed whether the government had to hold a public inquiry.

key_atrocities.name: Sungai Siput killings
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Malayan Communist Party / Malayan National Liberation Army
key_atrocities.notes: Three European estate managers were killed in Perak on 16 June 1948, precipitating the Emergency declaration.

key_atrocities.name: Bukit Kepong attack
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Malayan Communist Party / Malayan National Liberation Army
key_atrocities.victims_group: police, auxiliary police, family members, and civilians
key_atrocities.notes: Royal Malaysian Police commemorations give 25 total dead, including police, auxiliary police, six family members, and two civilians.

uncertainty_notes: The official casualty tables are strong for aggregate killed/missing figures but weak for perpetrator-coded civilian deaths. The MCP/MNLA civilian-death range is therefore residual, derived from total civilian deaths minus confirmed or confirmed+suspicious British/security-force civilian killings. Indirect deaths from government resettlement, detention, deportation, food control, and Orang Asli relocation are qualitatively documented but not responsibly quantifiable from the sources found, so they are not added to the grand total.
